**Distinction Bias: Misprediction and Mischoice Due to Joint Evaluation** — Hsee & Zhang (2004)  
When options are compared side by side, people overweight attributes that are easy to contrast and underweight how the option will feel when lived separately. This supports caution about overvaluing salary, title, or team size relative to day-to-day interest.

**Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction** — Schkade & Kahneman (1998)  
People overestimate the importance of salient differences when predicting happiness. In job choice, conspicuous offer features can crowd out less salient but repeatedly experienced factors like task interest and colleagues.

**A Survey Method for Characterizing Daily Life Experience: The Day Reconstruction Method** — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2004)  
Subjective well-being is strongly shaped by what people actually do during the day and who they do it with. This makes the texture of daily work more important than a one-time comparison of compensation or status.

**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham (1976)  
Autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. Interesting work matters, but the evidence favors a broader “good job design” bundle rather than interest alone.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** — Ryan & Deci (2000)  
Motivation and well-being are supported by autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Intrinsic interest is central, but leadership, team fit, and autonomy can also satisfy core psychological needs.

**Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis** — Hoff, Song, Wee, Phan & Rounds (2020)  
Fit between a person’s interests and their work environment is reliably associated with job satisfaction. The effect is meaningful but not so large that it justifies ignoring pay, culture, autonomy, or career trajectory.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)  
People who experience their work as a calling report higher work and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career. This supports weighting meaning and deep interest heavily, especially in mission-driven technical work.

**A Meta-Analytic Examination of the Relationship Between Job Satisfaction and Subjective Well-Being** — Bowling, Eschleman & Wang (2010)  
Job satisfaction and overall subjective well-being are substantially related. Because work takes up so much waking life, sacrificing daily work quality can plausibly affect life satisfaction.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)  
Pay level is positively but only modestly related to job satisfaction. This suggests a small salary increase is unlikely to dominate differences in work content, autonomy, or fit.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023)  
Emotional well-being generally rises with income, though the pattern is heterogeneous and diminishing for some people. The evidence argues against ignoring salary, but not for prioritizing a modest salary bump over large experiential differences.

**Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do** — Amabile (1997)  
Intrinsic motivation is a major driver of creativity, especially for complex and open-ended work. For research roles, interesting problems may affect not only happiness but also the quality of output.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999)  
Expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation when they feel controlling. Compensation is not inherently bad, but moving mainly for extrinsic rewards may be risky if it reduces autonomy or interest.

**A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Person–Organization Fit and Work Attitudes** — Verquer, Beehr & Wagner (2003)  
Fit with an organization’s values and culture predicts satisfaction, commitment, and lower turnover intentions. The relevant comparison is not just interesting versus less interesting work, but also which institution feels more aligned.

**Motivation to Lead: A Meta-Analysis and Distal-Proximal Model of Motivation and Leadership** — Badura, Grijalva, Galvin, Owens & Joseph (2020)  
Motivation to lead predicts leadership emergence and leadership outcomes. Wanting to lead a small team can be an intrinsic preference of its own, not merely a status or compensation consideration.

**Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?** — O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018)  
People who believe interests must be “found” are less likely to persist when initially appealing paths become difficult. This cautions against treating current interestingness as fixed, since leadership and ownership can make initially less interesting work become more engaging.

- **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.** Shows that autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback predict internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports weighting “interesting work” heavily, especially when it also gives autonomy and visible impact.

- **The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis — Fried & Ferris, 1987.** Finds that enriched, intrinsically motivating jobs are reliably associated with higher satisfaction, motivation, and lower absenteeism. The effects on objective performance are positive but less consistent, so interesting work matters but is not the only career variable.

- **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.** Finds intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance, especially quality and complex work. Extrinsic incentives also predict performance, especially quantity, so the evidence favors “interesting work matters a lot,” not “ignore everything else.”

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.** Finds that expected, contingent rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. This supports caution about choosing mainly for salary or status if it makes the work feel controlled or less self-directed.

- **Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity — Amabile et al., 1996.** Finds that creativity is supported by challenging work, autonomy, resources, supervisory encouragement, and supportive teams. For research-heavy roles, “interestingness” is partly the work itself and partly the environment that lets creative progress happen.

- **The Work Design Questionnaire: Developing and Validating a Comprehensive Measure for Assessing Job Design and the Nature of Work — Morgeson & Humphrey, 2006.** Shows that jobs differ across task, knowledge, social, and contextual features, including autonomy, complexity, feedback, interdependence, and social support. A leadership role may be “less technically interesting” but could still be motivating if it increases autonomy, influence, learning, and feedback.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.** Finds that fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. This suggests the key question is not just “interesting vs. salary,” but which role better fits your motives, values, abilities, and desired trajectory.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997.** Distinguishes people who see work as a job, career, or calling, and finds calling-oriented workers report especially high work and life satisfaction. This supports the idea that meaningful, intrinsically engaging work can be unusually important for some people.

- **The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work — Bunderson & Thompson, 2009.** Finds that seeing work as a calling brings meaning and identity, but also makes people willing to accept lower pay, hardship, and exploitation. This is a caution against focusing on interesting or meaningful work “almost exclusively.”

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.** Finds that pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. This supports the claim that salary is often overweighted, though pay satisfaction and perceived fairness still matter.

- **Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006.** Argues that people often overestimate the effect of income on happiness because money is highly salient when making comparisons. This is relevant to job offers because salary differences can loom larger during decision-making than they feel in day-to-day work.

- **Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year — Killingsworth, 2021.** Finds that experienced well-being continues to rise with income on a roughly log-linear basis, with no sharp plateau in the sampled range. This complicates the “ignore salary” view: money may be less important than interesting work for many people, but it is not irrelevant.

Workplace satisfaction and performance improve when employees feel autonomous, competent, and connected to others. A less intrinsically fascinating role can still be fulfilling if it offers leadership, mastery, and strong relationships.

**“The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art” — Bakker & Demerouti (2007).**

**“A Meta-Analysis of Work Demand Stressors and Job Performance” — LePine, Podsakoff & LePine (2005).**

**“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010).**  
Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is relatively modest. A higher salary helps, but it rarely compensates fully for poor fit, low autonomy, or uninteresting work.

**“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers (2023).**

People often mispredict which job attributes will make them happy when comparing options abstractly. This is relevant because salary, title, and team leadership may be salient now, while day-to-day intellectual engagement may dominate later.

**“Affective Forecasting” — Wilson & Gilbert (2003).**  
People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of future emotional reactions. When choosing between roles, it helps to simulate ordinary workdays rather than focus only on headline attributes like pay, prestige, or novelty.

**“The Pursuit of Happiness Can Be Lonely” — Mauss et al. (2012).**  
Overemphasizing a single route to happiness can backfire. Applied to careers, optimizing almost exclusively for “interestingness” may be risky if it crowds out relationships, stability, health, growth, or values.

**“Subjective Well-Being and Income: Is There Any Evidence of Satiation?” — Jebb et al. (2018).**  
This study finds that income’s relationship with well-being varies by region and by type of well-being, with possible satiation points for some measures. It supports treating salary as important but not automatically decisive.

**“Managers and Leaders: Are They Different?” — Zaleznik (1977).**

**“Leadership in Organizations” — Yukl (various editions; research synthesis).**  
Leadership roles can provide influence, development, and meaning, but also introduce coordination burdens, conflict, and reduced individual technical work. If the Anthropic role is “less interesting,” the tradeoff is whether leadership itself is intrinsically interesting to you.**

**“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” — Gagné & Deci (2005)**  
Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central predictors of high-quality motivation at work. This supports weighting intrinsically interesting work heavily, but not “almost exclusively,” because team fit, autonomy, and growth also matter.

**“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
Jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. “Interesting work” is partly captured here, but the theory says job design is multidimensional rather than reducible to interestingness alone.

**“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
Fit with the job, organization, team, and manager predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover. This suggests the right question is not only “which work is more interesting?” but also “where do my abilities, values, collaborators, and role fit best?”

**“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds, & Drasgow (2012)**  
People tend to perform better and persist more when their interests match their work. This is strong evidence that interestingness is not just a luxury; it can affect long-run productivity and success.

**“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
Meaningful work is associated with higher job satisfaction, engagement, organizational commitment, life satisfaction, and performance. The evidence supports prioritizing meaning and interest, though much of it is correlational rather than definitive causal proof.

**“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
Pay level has a positive but relatively weak relationship with job satisfaction. This argues against over-weighting a modest salary increase, especially when both options already pay well.

**“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023)**  
Experienced well-being generally rises with income, but the relationship is roughly logarithmic and varies across people. Money matters, but the evidence does not imply that a somewhat higher salary should dominate daily work quality.

**“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999)**  
Tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when experienced as controlling. This supports caution about trading intrinsically compelling work for external rewards like salary or title.

**“Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity” — Amabile, Conti, Coon, Lazenby, & Herron (1996)**  
Creative work is supported by challenging tasks, autonomy, resources, encouragement, and low organizational impediments. For a research role, the interestingness and creative affordances of the environment may be central to future output.

**“Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want” — Wilson & Gilbert (2005)**  
People often mispredict how future circumstances will affect their happiness, especially by over-focusing on salient changes. Salary, title, and company switch are salient, while the day-to-day texture of interesting work may be underweighted.

High individual-contributor performance can predict promotion even when it does not predict managerial performance. A team-lead opportunity should therefore be evaluated as a different craft and daily job, not automatically as career progress.

**“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw, & Stanton (2015)**  
Boss quality has large effects on worker productivity, partly through teaching and improving team output. If leading a team is genuinely motivating and you expect to be good at it, management can be an intrinsically meaningful path rather than merely an external perk.

**“Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion” — Vallerand et al. (2003)**  
Harmonious passion is associated with well-being and sustained engagement, while obsessive passion is associated with conflict and rigidity. This is a caution against focusing “almost exclusively” on interestingness if doing so crowds out balance, relationships, or broader values.

answer:
**The Experience Matters More Than You Think: People Value Intrinsic Incentives More Inside Than Outside an Activity** — Woolley & Fishbach  
People tend to underweight how much the immediate experience of an activity will matter when choosing, relative to external outcomes. This supports the idea that “interesting day-to-day work” is often underrated, but not that other job attributes should be ignored.

**For the Fun of It: Harnessing Immediate Rewards to Increase Persistence in Long-Term Goals** — Woolley & Fishbach  
Immediate rewards such as enjoyment predict persistence in long-term goals more strongly than delayed rewards. This suggests that work you find intrinsically engaging may be especially important for sustained effort and motivation.

**Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham  
The Job Characteristics Model finds that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict internal motivation and satisfaction. Interesting work matters, but the model treats autonomy, impact, and feedback as equally central.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson  
This meta-analysis finds that motivational job features strongly relate to satisfaction, motivation, and performance, while social support and work context also matter. The evidence supports prioritizing intrinsically motivating work, but not focusing on it almost exclusively.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** — Ryan & Deci  
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. A technically less interesting role could still be fulfilling if it offers leadership, mastery, and strong relationships.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner & Ryan  
Controlling tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. This supports protecting intrinsic interest, but it does not imply that salary, status, or promotion are inherently bad.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow  
Vocational interests predict job performance, training performance, and persistence, especially when interests fit the work environment. This is strong evidence that doing work aligned with genuine interests affects outcomes, not just happiness.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson  
Person–job fit strongly predicts satisfaction, commitment, and intent to stay, while organization, group, and supervisor fit also matter. Interesting work is therefore important, but team, culture, manager, and values fit can be similarly consequential.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich  
Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction and pay satisfaction. This suggests that a small salary increase should usually not dominate job-content considerations, though compensation still matters for security and fairness.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers  
Income is positively related to well-being for most people, but the gains are heterogeneous and often modest at higher incomes. This argues against ignoring salary, but also against treating a somewhat higher salary as decisive.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion** — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone  
People often overestimate the effect of income differences on overall happiness because income becomes salient during comparison. This supports skepticism toward overweighting compensation relative to daily lived experience.

**Affect and Creativity at Work** — Amabile, Barsade, Mueller & Staw  
Positive affect at work is associated with greater creativity, including evidence that mood can precede creative thought. For research roles, intrinsically engaging work may matter because it supports the emotional conditions for creative output.

**The Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review** — Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski  
Meaningful work comes from authenticity, competence, purpose, belonging, and contribution to others. Leading a team could therefore create meaning through mentorship and impact, even if the technical work is less intrinsically interesting.

**Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead** — Chan & Drasgow  
Motivation to lead is a meaningful individual-difference variable associated with leadership participation and potential. If leading a small team is something you have long wanted, that desire is a relevant intrinsic-fit signal rather than merely an extrinsic perk.

**Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?** — O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton  
People who see interests as fixed are less open to new domains and more likely to expect effortless motivation from a “passion.” This cautions against optimizing only for current interestingness, because interests can develop or fade depending on context.

**“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman and Oldham (1976)**  
This paper tests the Job Characteristics Model, where skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy and feedback predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. It supports taking interesting, meaningful, autonomous work very seriously, but defines “interesting” as a bundle of design features rather than novelty alone.

**“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang and Morgeson (2007)**  
This meta-analysis finds that motivational work-design features, especially autonomy, task significance, feedback and skill variety, are strongly related to satisfaction, motivation and performance. It also finds that social and contextual features matter, so role content is central but not the only variable.

**“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan and Deci (2000)**  
Self-determination theory argues that autonomy, competence and relatedness are basic psychological needs that drive well-being and sustained motivation. A less technically interesting role can still be motivating if it strongly increases autonomy, mastery and connection.

**“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner and Ryan (1999)**  
This meta-analysis finds that tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks when they feel controlling. It supports skepticism toward salary or status as substitutes for intrinsically engaging work, while not implying that pay is irrelevant.

**“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin and Ford (2014)**  
Across many studies, intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives both predict performance, with intrinsic motivation especially important for quality and complex work. This weakens the “almost exclusively” claim: interest matters a lot, but incentives and context still matter.

**“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman and Johnson (2005)**  
Fit with the job, organization, group and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance and turnover intentions. The evidence favors optimizing overall fit rather than maximizing only intellectual interest.

**“Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis” — Hoff, Song, Wee, Phan and Rounds (2020)**  
This meta-analysis finds that people are more satisfied when their vocational interests match their work. The effect is meaningful but not overwhelming, suggesting that interest should be heavily weighted but not treated as the sole criterion.

**“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds and Drasgow (2012)**  
This review finds that interest fit predicts performance, persistence and achievement across work and educational settings. For research-heavy careers, doing work you find genuinely interesting can compound into better output and longer-run career capital.

**“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw and Rich (2010)**  
This meta-analysis finds that pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. It suggests that a modest salary increase may matter less than work design, fit and autonomy, especially once baseline financial needs are comfortably met.

**“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman and Mellers (2023)**  
This paper reconciles earlier conflicting findings and concludes that emotional well-being generally continues rising with income, though with heterogeneity and diminishing practical salience for many people. It argues against saying money does not matter, but also against treating money as dominant over day-to-day work experience.

**“Job Demands, Job Decision Latitude, and Mental Strain: Implications for Job Redesign” — Karasek (1979)**  
This paper shows that high demands are most harmful when paired with low decision latitude, while high-demand, high-control jobs can be stimulating and growth-producing. A team-lead role may improve well-being if it brings real control, but may hurt if it mainly adds coordination burden.

**“Linking Job Demands and Resources to Employee Engagement and Burnout: A Theoretical Extension and Meta-Analytic Test” — Crawford, LePine and Rich (2010)**  
This meta-analysis distinguishes challenge demands, which can increase engagement, from hindrance demands, which tend to increase burnout and reduce motivation. Leading a small team could be positive if it feels like responsibility and growth, but negative if it becomes bureaucracy and role conflict.

**“Leadership Is Associated with Lower Levels of Stress” — Sherman, Lee, Cuddy, Renshon, Oveis, Gross and Lerner (2012)**  
This study finds that leaders report lower stress and show lower cortisol than nonleaders, partly because leadership increases perceived control. It suggests that management responsibility can be a well-being resource when authority is real rather than merely nominal.

**“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin and Schwartz (1997)**  
This paper distinguishes people who see work mainly as a job, career or calling, with calling orientation associated with greater satisfaction. It supports the importance of meaningful work, but meaning can come from impact, leadership and mission as well as from technical interest.

answer:
- **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.**  
  Jobs with autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback produce more internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports taking “interesting work” seriously when it means challenging, autonomous, high-feedback work.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007.**  
  This meta-analysis finds that work design features such as autonomy, task significance, feedback, complexity, and social support are strongly related to satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It suggests day-to-day role content often matters more than people expect.

- **Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover — Van Iddekinge et al., 2011.**  
  People whose jobs fit their interests tend to perform better and are less likely to leave. This is direct evidence that interest fit is not just a luxury preference.

- **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.**  
  Intrinsic motivation is especially predictive of performance quality, while incentives are more predictive of performance quantity. The evidence supports weighting intrinsic interest heavily, but not “almost exclusively.”

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.**  
  Expected, controlling rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. Higher pay is not psychologically irrelevant, but its effect depends on whether it feels controlling, fair, or enabling.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature — Judge et al., 2010.**  
  Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. This suggests salary matters, but usually less than role content, autonomy, fit, and relationships.

- **Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion — Kahneman et al., 2006.**  
  People tend to overestimate how much income will affect their experienced well-being when they are explicitly thinking about money. This supports the idea that salary can be over-weighted in career decisions.

- **Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.**  
  The paper finds that experienced well-being generally rises with income, though patterns differ by baseline happiness and may flatten for less-happy people. This weakens simple “money stops mattering” claims while still suggesting income is only one input.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person-Job, Person-Organization, Person-Group, and Person-Supervisor Fit — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.**  
  Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. A less intellectually exciting role could still be better if it strongly fits your values, desired identity, and leadership goals.

- **Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis — Allan et al., 2019.**  
  Meaningful work is strongly associated with engagement, job satisfaction, life satisfaction, commitment, and lower withdrawal. The evidence supports prioritizing meaning and interest, but “meaningful” includes impact, values, and purpose—not just technical fascination.

- **The Significance of Task Significance — Grant, 2008.**  
  Increasing workers’ sense that their work benefits others improves persistence and performance. This suggests the perceived impact of each role may matter as much as whether the work is intellectually interesting.

- **The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work — Bunderson & Thompson, 2009.**  
  People with strong callings often experience their work as deeply meaningful but also accept lower pay, sacrifice, and difficult conditions. This cautions against focusing on interesting or meaningful work “almost exclusively.”

- **The Value of Bosses — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton, 2015.**  
  Good bosses substantially improve worker productivity, showing that management quality has real leverage. If leading a small team is a core developmental goal, the leadership opportunity is not merely an extrinsic perk.

- **Promotions and the Peter Principle — Benson, Li & Shue, 2019.**  
  High-performing individual contributors are often promoted into management, but strong IC performance does not necessarily predict strong managerial performance. This is relevant because “more leadership” may be valuable, but it is a different job with different success conditions.
